title: Ecclesiastes 11
subtitle: Cast Your Bread; Rejoice, Young Person

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — “Cast Your Bread on the Waters”

heb.paragraph: "Cast (shallaḥ) your bread on the waters, for after many days you will find it again" (v.1). The image is of maritime trade: send grain overseas and the profit will return eventually. Qoheleth’s counsel: invest broadly, act generously, take risks — because you cannot predict which ventures will succeed.

refs.note: "Whoever sows generously will also reap generously" — Paul’s sowing theology echoes Qoheleth.
"Let us not become weary in doing good, for at the proper time we will reap" — sowing with patience.

notes.note: "Cast your bread on the waters... invest in seven ventures, yes, in eight." Diversify. Generosity and prudence combined: give broadly because you don’t know which investment will pay off.
"Whoever watches the wind will not plant; whoever looks at the clouds will not reap." Waiting for certainty prevents action. Faith means acting without complete information.

"Sow your seed in the morning, and at evening let your hands not be idle, for you do not know which will succeed." Work persistently because outcomes are unpredictable. Effort is your responsibility; results are God’s.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "bread on the waters" is commercial: sending grain by ship. The return is delayed but expected. Faith bridges the gap between sending and receiving.
Alter: the wind-watcher and cloud-gazer are paralysed by analysis. Qoheleth’s counter: imperfect action beats perfect inaction.
Calvin: generosity as investment is God’s economy. What is given away returns; what is hoarded rots. Calvin: the stingy person trusts their own management more than God’s provision.
Calvin: "you do not know the work of God" is the same epistemological limit as 3:11 and 8:17, but now applied positively: since you don’t know, sow everywhere and let God choose which prospers.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "after many days you will find it again" suggests a delayed return, not guaranteed timing. The investment is an act of faith.
The NET: "the work of God who makes everything" — divine sovereignty undergirds the counsel to act. God is working even when you cannot see how.

longman.source: Tremper Longman III, Ecclesiastes, NICOT (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Longman: the bread-on-water image combines generosity with business acumen. Qoheleth counsels bold action precisely because the future is unknown. Hebel drives generosity, not paralysis.
Longman: "you do not know which will succeed" — the ignorance that frustrated Qoheleth in earlier chapters now motivates him. Since you cannot know, try everything.

fox.source: Michael V. Fox, Ecclesiastes, JPS (2004) / Proverbs, Anchor Bible — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fox: this section reverses Qoheleth’s earlier tone. After chapters of "you cannot know," he now says: "therefore act." The practical response to epistemological limits is not withdrawal but broad engagement.
Fox: "the work of God who makes everything" — Qoheleth grounds his counsel in theology: God is at work in the unknown. Your ignorance is not God’s absence.

hist.context: After chapters of observation and warning, Qoheleth shifts to counsel for action. Cast your bread (v.1), distribute to seven or eight (v.2), sow in the morning and evening (v.6). The logic: since you cannot predict the future, act broadly rather than narrowly. Do not let uncertainty paralyse you. The wind and clouds are unpredictable (vv.4–5), but the farmer who waits for perfect conditions never sows.

sections.header: Verses 7–10 — “Light Is Sweet”; Rejoice, Young Person

heb.paragraph: "Rejoice, young person, in your youth (baḥūrōṭek̅ā), and let your heart give you joy in the days of your youth" (v.9). The seventh and final "enjoy" passage. Directed specifically at the young: use your strength, follow your heart, enjoy your eyes — but know that God will bring you to judgment. The joy is real; the accountability is also real.

refs.note: "Don’t let anyone look down on you because you are young" — the same affirmation of youth.
"Take delight in the LORD, and he will give you the desires of your heart" — the Psalm that complements Qoheleth’s "follow your heart."

notes.note: "Light is sweet, and it pleases the eyes to see the sun. However many years anyone may live, let them enjoy them all." Simple gratitude for existence. Even Qoheleth, after all his hebel, says: being alive is good.

"Rejoice, young person... follow the ways of your heart and whatever your eyes see, but know that for all these things God will bring you into judgment." The balance: enjoy fully AND live accountably. Joy and judgment are not opposites.

"Banish anxiety from your heart and cast off the troubles of your body, for youth and vigour are meaningless." Youth itself is hebel — not because it is bad but because it is brief. Therefore: don’t waste it on worry.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "light is sweet" — monosyllabic, unqualified, beautiful. After 10 chapters of complexity, Qoheleth’s simplest sentence is his most memorable.

Alter: "follow your heart and your eyes" inverts the warning of Numbers 15:39 ("do not follow after your own heart and your own eyes"). Qoheleth is more permissive than Torah — but the judgment clause restores the boundary.

Calvin: "God will bring you into judgment" is the eschatological frame that redeems the carpe diem counsel. Enjoy freely, but not as if there were no Judge. The balance is freedom within accountability.
Calvin: "banish anxiety" is a command, not a suggestion. Anxiety dishonours the God who gives today’s portion. Calvin: worry is the opposite of faith, and Qoheleth condemns it alongside folly.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "God will bring you into judgment" is debated — is it Qoheleth’s own caveat or a later editorial addition? The text as it stands frames joy within divine accountability.
The NET: "youth and vigour" (yaldūṭ) literally means "dawn of life." The metaphor: youth is the morning of existence, bright but brief.

longman.source: Tremper Longman III, Ecclesiastes, NICOT (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Longman: "follow your heart" is not antinomian permission but death-aware urgency. Enjoy deeply because the window is closing. The judgment clause prevents the counsel from becoming licence.
Longman: "youth and vigour are hebel" is the transition to chapter 12’s aging allegory. The best years pass fastest. What follows — aging and death — comes for everyone.

fox.source: Michael V. Fox, Ecclesiastes, JPS (2004) / Proverbs, Anchor Bible — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fox: "light is sweet" is Qoheleth’s simplest positive statement. No qualification, no "but." Life is good. This is not inconsistent with hebel — the transient can still be sweet.

Fox: the judgment clause is either Qoheleth’s own caveat or the epilogue narrator’s addition. Either way, it frames the joy within accountability. Fox: the tension is the book’s final word on enjoyment — real, encouraged, and answerable.

hist.context: The book’s tone lifts. "Light is sweet" (v.7) — simply being alive is good. Then the direct address to youth: rejoice, follow your heart, walk in the sight of your eyes. But: "know that for all these things God will bring you into judgment" (v.9). The joy is encouraged but framed by accountability. And: "banish anxiety from your heart and cast off the troubles of your body, for youth and vigour are hebel" (v.10). Even the best days are fleeting. This prepares for the aging allegory in chapter 12.

ppl.text: But: "know that for all these things God will bring you into judgment" (v…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHowever many years anyone may live, let them enjoy them all. But let them remember the days of darkness, for there will be many. Everything to come is meaningless.ESVSo if a person lives many years, let him rejoice in them all; but let him remember that the days of darkness will be many. All that comes is vanity.NIVIf clouds are full of water, they pour rain on the earth. Whether a tree falls to the south or to the north, in the place where it falls, there it will lie.ESVIf the clouds are full of rain, they empty themselves on the earth, and if a tree falls to the south or to the north, in the place where the tree falls, there it will lie.

rec.text: ""Cast your bread on the waters... invest in seven ventures, yes, in eight." Diversify. Generosity and prudence combined: give broadly because you don’t know which investment will pay off."

rows.label: Cast Your Bread — 11:1–6
rows.text: Invest broadly. Sow morning and evening. Act despite uncertainty.

hebtext.note: "Cast (shallaḥ) your bread on the waters, for after many days you will find it again" (v.1). The image is of maritime trade: send grain overseas and the profit will return eventually. Qoheleth’s counsel: invest broadly, act generously, take risks — because you cannot predict which ventures will succeed.

"Rejoice, young person, in your youth (baḥūrōṭek̅ā), and let your heart give you joy in the days of your youth" (v.9). The seventh and final "enjoy" passage. Directed specifically at the young: use your strength, follow your heart, enjoy your eyes — but know that God will bring you to judgment. The joy is real; the accountability is also real.

themes.note: Mercy peaks: "light is sweet," the carpe diem counsel, the warmest "enjoy" passage. Faith: acting despite uncertainty. Judgment: accountability frames the joy.
